Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Spectral Diagnostics for Deterministic Mixed-Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that layer-wise precision assignment can be deterministically routed via pre-execution spectral diagnostics of the Hessian eigenvalue decay and gradient covariance conditioning, without requiring training-time calibration or per-token adaptive overhead. Specifically, layers exhibiting steep Hessian eigenvalue decay (low effective dimensionality) tolerate lower precision, while layers with slow decay (high feature entanglement) require higher precision. By formalizing this routing via explicit condition number thresholds and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds, we can achieve provably stable mixed-precision inference with zero training overhead and bounded per-layer diagnostic cost.
Related Work: Recent work on spectral latent steering (SLS) and test-time optimization demonstrates the power of zero-training spectral subspace methods. However, existing mixed-precision routing methods (e.g., learned quantization, sensitivity-based heuristics) either require training-time calibration, incur per-token adaptive overhead, or lack formal stability guarantees. Unlike prior work that treats precision assignment as a learned or heuristic problem, this proposal operationalizes precision routing as a deterministic spectral diagnostic problem grounded in optimization theory. We distinguish from prior work by: (1) coupling Hessian eigenvalue decay directly to precision requirements via explicit condition number bounds; (2) replacing per-token adaptive routing with block-level pre-execution diagnostics; (3) providing PAC-Bayes generalization certificates for precision-switched inference; (4) enforcing strict mathematical accounting of diagnostic overhead via spectral rank truncation.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises efficiency gains, yet existing methods rely on opaque heuristics or expensive training-time calibration. We propose a training-free, deterministic framework for layer-wise precision assignment grounded in spectral geometry and optimization stability theory. Our key insight is that the Hessian eigenvalue decay profile—quantifiable via a single forward pass on representative inputs—directly predicts precision tolerance: layers with steep decay (low effective dimensionality) can safely use lower precision, while layers with slow decay require higher precision to maintain stability. We formalize this via explicit condition number thresholds and derive PAC-Bayes generalization bounds that certify inference stability under precision switching. Our framework performs coarse-grained, block-level diagnostics rather than per-token routing, ensuring bounded computational overhead (< 2% amortized cost). We validate on language models (LLaMA, Mistral) and vision transformers, demonstrating that spectral-routing-based precision assignment matches or exceeds learned quantization baselines while providing formal stability guarantees. Crucially, our method requires zero training, operates deterministically, and produces interpretable routing decisions anchored in linear algebra rather than opaque learned parameters.

Experiments.name: Spectral Diagnostic Validation
Experiments.description: For each layer in a pre-trained transformer, compute: (1) Hessian eigenvalue spectrum via power iteration on a batch of representative inputs; (2) gradient covariance matrix rank and condition number; (3) effective dimensionality via cumulative eigenvalue ratio. Establish empirical thresholds: layers where top-k eigenvalues explain >95% of variance are designated 'low-curvature' (candidate for lower precision); layers requiring >100 eigenvalues for 95% variance are 'high-curvature' (require higher precision). Metric: measure correlation between Hessian decay profile and actual per-layer quantization sensitivity via gradient perturbation experiments.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For layer l with input X ∈ ℝ^{B×d}, compute Hessian-vector products via finite differences: H_approx = (∇²_l L) estimated using 2-3 forward-backward passes. Apply randomized eigenvalue decomposition (Halko et al.) truncated to rank k=min(50, d/4) to bound cost. Compute condition number κ_l = λ_max/λ_min. Assign precision tier: if κ_l < 100, use FP8; if 100 ≤ κ_l < 500, use FP16; if κ_l ≥ 500, use BF16.
Experiments.metrics: Spearman correlation between Hessian condition number and layer-wise quantization error; per-layer Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) preservation under assigned precision

Experiments.name: Deterministic Routing vs. Learned Baselines
Experiments.description: Compare spectral-routing precision assignment against: (1) uniform precision (FP16, FP8); (2) learned quantization (QAT, BRECQ); (3) sensitivity-based heuristics (gradient norm ranking). For each method, measure: (a) inference accuracy on standard benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag for LLMs; ImageNet for ViTs); (b) amortized diagnostic overhead (wall-clock time for spectral computation + inference); (c) end-to-end model size and throughput.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Implement spectral routing as pre-execution diagnostic loop: (i) load model; (ii) perform single forward pass on 32-token representative batch; (iii) compute Hessian eigenvalue spectrum for each layer using randomized SVD (cost O(d²) per layer, amortized <2% of inference time); (iv) assign precision tiers based on condition number thresholds; (v) quantize weights/activations; (vi) run inference. Track wall-clock overhead explicitly.
Experiments.metrics: Task accuracy (%) under each precision scheme; amortized diagnostic cost (% of total inference latency); model size (MB); tokens-per-second throughput

Experiments.name: PAC-Bayes Generalization Certificates
Experiments.description: Derive formal generalization bounds for precision-switched inference. For a layer quantized to lower precision, bound the test loss increase via PAC-Bayes theory. Specifically, relate the precision-induced perturbation δ_p (quantization noise) to the Hessian condition number κ and margin γ. Establish: with high probability, test loss increase ≤ O(δ_p · κ / γ). Validate this bound empirically by measuring actual test loss increase vs. predicted bound across multiple datasets.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For layer l with quantization perturbation δ_p bounded by precision tier, compute: (1) margin γ = min |y_i - f(x_i)| across validation set; (2) Hessian condition number κ_l from spectral diagnostics; (3) PAC-Bayes bound B_l = C · δ_p · κ_l / γ (where C is a universal constant). Aggregate bounds across layers: total bound B_total = Σ_l B_l. Empirically verify by measuring actual test loss increase and comparing to B_total.
Experiments.metrics: Tightness of PAC-Bayes bound (ratio of predicted bound to actual loss increase); fraction of test samples where bound holds; variance of bound across different random seeds

Experiments.name: Ablation: Diagnostic Granularity
Experiments.description: Test whether coarse-grained (block-level) diagnostics suffice vs. per-layer or per-head diagnostics. Compare: (1) single Hessian spectrum for entire layer; (2) separate spectra for attention vs. FFN blocks; (3) per-attention-head spectra. Measure accuracy preservation and diagnostic overhead for each variant.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Implement three routing variants: (i) Layer-level: one Hessian eigenvalue spectrum per transformer layer; (ii) Block-level: separate spectra for each attention and FFN block; (iii) Head-level: separate spectra per attention head. Measure diagnostic cost (wall-clock time) and final inference accuracy for each variant on a fixed precision budget.
Experiments.metrics: Inference accuracy (%) for each diagnostic granularity; amortized diagnostic overhead (% of inference latency); memory footprint of diagnostic caches

Experiments.name: Stability Radius Validation
Experiments.description: Empirically validate that layers assigned lower precision based on spectral routing remain within a formal stability radius. For each layer, compute the maximum allowable perturbation Δ before loss degrades beyond a threshold (e.g., 1% accuracy drop). Compare this to the quantization noise introduced by the assigned precision tier. Verify that quantization noise < stability radius.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For layer l: (1) compute Lipschitz constant L_l via spectral norm of Jacobian (upper bounded by Hessian condition number); (2) measure actual quantization noise σ_l under assigned precision; (3) predict stability radius R_l = ε / L_l (where ε is acceptable loss increase); (4) verify σ_l < R_l. Track violations and correlate with final accuracy drops.
Experiments.metrics: Fraction of layers where quantization noise < predicted stability radius; correlation between stability radius violation and accuracy degradation; per-layer margin of safety (R_l / σ_l ratio)

Experiments.name: Multi-Seed Robustness and Variance Quantification
Experiments.description: Run all experiments across 5 random seeds (different weight initializations, different representative input batches for diagnostics). Report 95% confidence intervals for all metrics. Verify that spectral routing decisions are stable across seeds and representative batch choices.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each seed: (1) load model; (2) randomly sample 32 representative tokens from dataset; (3) compute Hessian eigenvalue spectrum; (4) assign precision tiers; (5) measure inference accuracy on full test set. Aggregate results across seeds; compute mean, std, and 95% CI for all metrics.
Experiments.metrics: 95% confidence intervals for accuracy, throughput, diagnostic overhead across seeds; coefficient of variation for precision tier assignments; Spearman correlation of tier assignments across different representative batches

Risk Factors and Limitations: Hessian computation via finite differences or power iteration can be noisy; we mitigate via randomized SVD with rank truncation and multiple random projections, but sensitivity to batch composition remains. Risk: diagnostic instability on small or non-representative batches. Mitigation: aggregate diagnostics over multiple random batches and validate stability across seeds.

PAC-Bayes bounds may be loose in practice, limiting their predictive power for per-layer precision assignment. The union bound over all layers (Σ_l B_l) can become vacuous. Mitigation: empirically calibrate bounds via held-out validation data and use empirical margin estimates rather than worst-case bounds.

Spectral diagnostics assume smooth loss landscapes; highly non-convex regions or adversarial inputs may violate assumptions. Limitation: method optimized for typical in-distribution inference, not certified for adversarial robustness.

Coarse-grained block-level routing may miss fine-grained precision requirements within attention heads or FFN sub-layers. Trade-off: we prioritize computational efficiency (bounded diagnostic overhead) over per-head granularity.

Spectral conditioning depends on batch statistics; different batch compositions or input distributions may yield different Hessian spectra. Risk: routing decisions may not transfer across domains. Mitigation: validate on out-of-distribution test sets and measure robustness.

Amortized diagnostic cost assumed to be negligible relative to inference; on very small models or single-token inference, diagnostic overhead may dominate. Limitation: method most suitable for batch inference or multi-turn conversations where amortization is significant.

No formal guarantee that lower-precision layers maintain semantic correctness; only loss-based stability bounds. Risk: may accumulate errors across layers. Mitigation: validate via end-to-end task accuracy and multi-seed robustness checks.
